core_aspects.description: Formed from the cooling and solidification of magma or lava, igneous rocks can be found both above ground (like basalt) and below ground (like granite). They are typically hard and crystalline.
Created from the compaction and cementation of sediments over time, sedimentary rocks like limestone and sandstone often contain fossils and form in layers, usually in watery environments.
Formed when existing rocks are subjected to heat and pressure, metamorphic rocks like marble and slate have changed structure and mineral composition but remain solid.

The rock cycle explains how rocks transform from one type to another over time through processes like melting, cooling, erosion, and compaction, connecting igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks.

Each rock type is made of different minerals, which give rocks their color, hardness, and other properties. For example, quartz and feldspar are common in igneous rocks, while calcite is typical in sedimentary rocks.
